claims.claim_text: A platform team had etcd snapshots, GitOps, and a Velero Schedule, but a storage class migration corrupted a production billing database volume and exposed gaps in restore practice.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, opening paragraph
claims.anchor_text: A platform team at a growing software company believed it had Kubernetes disaster recovery covered because every cluster had etcd snapshots, GitOps repositories, and a Velero Schedule. Then a storage class migration corrupted the production billing database volume, and the incident commander learned that all three recovery mechanisms protected different pieces of the system. GitOps could recreate Deployments and Services, but it could not recreate runtime data. The etcd snapshot captured cluster state, but it was tied to the damaged control plane and still did not provide a clean application-level rollback. Velero had backups, but nobody had practiced restoring them into an isolated namespace with volume data attached.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A common disaster-recovery failure is assuming that several backup mechanisms protect the same thing. GitOps can recreate reviewed Kubernetes manifests, but it does not recreate runtime data. Etcd snapshots capture control-plane state, while application-level rollback and persistent data recovery require their own tested restore path. Velero backups only become useful operational evidence when teams practice restoring workloads, including any required volume data, into a controlled environment.
claims.rationale: The paragraph reads as a concrete incident but gives no source, named organization, or verifiable postmortem, so the incident specifics should be removed.

claims.claim_text: Velero can back up and restore Kubernetes cluster resources and persistent volumes, migrate resources to other clusters, and replicate production clusters to dev/test clusters.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters; section 1 recovery model
claims.rationale: The Velero README directly lists backup/restore, migration, and replication capabilities.

claims.claim_text: Velero consists of a server running in the cluster and a command-line client running locally.
claims.span_hint: section: 2. Install Velero and Choose the Backup Boundary
claims.rationale: The upstream README states Velero has a cluster server and local CLI client.

claims.claim_text: Velero Backup, Restore, Schedule, BackupStorageLocation, and VolumeSnapshotLocation are Velero custom resources used to drive backup and restore behavior.
claims.span_hint: section: 2. Install Velero and Choose the Backup Boundary
claims.rationale: Velero docs describe BackupStorageLocation and VolumeSnapshotLocation and the API type docs cover Backup, Restore, and Schedule.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes etcd data can be backed up with snapshots, and etcd restore procedures require careful control-plane handling.
claims.span_hint: section: 1. Build the Recovery Model Before Installing Velero
claims.rationale: Kubernetes documentation describes etcd snapshot backup and restore procedures.

claims.claim_text: Velero supports namespace, resource, label selector, and cluster-resource filtering for backups and restores.
claims.span_hint: section: 2. Install Velero and Choose the Backup Boundary
claims.rationale: The Velero resource filtering documentation directly lists these filtering mechanisms.

claims.claim_text: Velero Backup objects support TTL, storageLocation, volumeSnapshotLocations, snapshotVolumes, defaultVolumesToFsBackup, and hook fields.
claims.span_hint: section: 2 declarative Backup manifest; section 3 hooks example
claims.rationale: The Backup API type documentation enumerates these fields.

claims.claim_text: Velero Schedule creates repeated backups from a cron expression, uses a Backup template, and gives created backups their own TTL.
claims.span_hint: section: 2 Schedule explanation
claims.rationale: The Schedule API type documentation describes cron scheduling, templates, and TTL for backups created by schedules.

claims.claim_text: Deleting a Schedule does not automatically delete every Backup it already produced unless owner references are explicitly used.
claims.span_hint: section: 2 Schedule explanation
claims.rationale: The Schedule API documents useOwnerReferencesInBackup and warns that backups are deleted with the schedule only when that option is true.

claims.claim_text: The module calls velero-plugin-for-aws:v1.13.0 a current AWS plugin family compatible with recent Velero releases.
claims.span_hint: section: 2 AWS install paragraph before velero install command
claims.anchor_text: The command below uses a current AWS plugin family compatible with recent Velero releases, but production teams should verify the compatibility matrix during upgrades.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The command below pins an AWS plugin version for the example; production teams should verify the Velero/plugin compatibility matrix during upgrades and adjust the tag to match the Velero server version.
claims.rationale: The current AWS plugin compatibility table shows v1.13.x maps to Velero v1.17.x, while current Velero v1.18 upgrade docs use AWS plugin v1.14.0.

claims.claim_text: The AWS Velero plugin persists backup data to S3 and creates/restores EBS snapshots, and v1.13.x is compatible with Velero v1.17.x.
claims.span_hint: section: 2 AWS plugin discussion
claims.rationale: The AWS plugin README directly states S3/EBS behavior and includes the compatibility matrix.

claims.claim_text: Velero supports provider plugins for AWS S3/EBS, Google Cloud Storage/GCE disks, Azure Blob/Azure Managed Disks, vSphere, and S3-compatible object stores including MinIO.
claims.span_hint: section: 2 storage provider plugin paragraph; Hands-On Step 3
claims.rationale: The supported providers documentation lists these object store and volume snapshot provider integrations.

claims.claim_text: BackupStorageLocation represents a bucket or prefix where Velero data is stored, and its Available phase indicates readiness.
claims.span_hint: section: 2 verify storage readiness
claims.rationale: The locations documentation defines BackupStorageLocation and describes checking for the Available phase.

claims.claim_text: Velero backup metadata and snapshot/volume data are separate; object storage holds metadata while volume snapshots or FSB hold persistent volume data.
claims.span_hint: section: 2 architecture diagram; Did You Know item 2
claims.rationale: The Velero locations documentation states backups are split into metadata in object storage and snapshots/backups of persistent volume data.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes VolumeSnapshot, VolumeSnapshotContent, and VolumeSnapshotClass are CRDs, and VolumeSnapshot support is only available for CSI drivers.
claims.span_hint: section: 3 volume approach discussion
claims.rationale: Kubernetes storage documentation directly documents VolumeSnapshot API objects and CSI driver requirements.

claims.claim_text: Velero CSI integration backs up and restores CSI-backed volumes using Kubernetes CSI Snapshot APIs, with built-in CSI support from Velero v1.14 onward.
claims.span_hint: section: 3 CSI snapshot discussion
claims.rationale: The Velero CSI documentation states it uses Kubernetes CSI Snapshot APIs and that the CSI plugin was merged into Velero from v1.14.

claims.claim_text: Velero File System Backup backs up and restores pod volumes through node agents using restic/kopia modules, is not tied to a specific storage platform, and is less point-in-time consistent than snapshots.
claims.span_hint: section: 3 file-system backup discussion
claims.rationale: The File System Backup documentation describes the node-agent mechanism, storage portability, and consistency limitations.

claims.claim_text: Velero can discover FSB volumes by opt-in pod annotation backup.velero.io/backup-volumes or by opt-out/default-volumes-to-fs-backup behavior.
claims.span_hint: section: 3 per-pod annotations and default FSB install
claims.rationale: The FSB documentation directly describes opt-in annotations and opt-out/default volume backup behavior.

claims.claim_text: Velero backup hooks can execute pre- and post-backup commands inside containers, including via pod annotations or Backup spec hooks.
claims.span_hint: section: 3 application hooks
claims.rationale: The backup hooks documentation explains pre/post command execution and the supported configuration mechanisms.

claims.claim_text: Velero Restore supports namespace mappings, resource include/exclude filters, restorePVs, existing resource policy, and restore hooks.
claims.span_hint: section: 3 restore operations
claims.rationale: The Restore API type documentation lists these restore configuration fields.

claims.claim_text: Velero restores can finish with status Completed while still reporting warnings or errors.
claims.span_hint: section: 3 restore validation; Common Mistakes
claims.rationale: Velero debugging documentation explicitly states a Restore may be Completed while warnings and errors are shown separately.

claims.claim_text: Velero restores CRDs before many dependent resources, with a documented default restore order.
claims.span_hint: section: 1 failure scenario table, CRD deleted before custom resources
claims.rationale: The restore reference documents the default restore order starting with Custom Resource Definitions.

claims.claim_text: Backups may contain Kubernetes Secrets and Velero itself does not encrypt backup metadata or snapshot data, relying on object/snapshot system mechanisms except for FSB details.
claims.span_hint: section: 1 backup security artifact paragraph; section: Operate Velero as a Reliability Service
claims.rationale: Velero locations documentation states Velero backups are split across metadata and volume data and that Velero itself does not encrypt them.

claims.claim_text: Velero exposes Prometheus metrics such as velero_backup_failure_total, velero_backup_success_total, restore metrics, and snapshot metrics.
claims.span_hint: section: 5 monitoring PrometheusRule example
claims.rationale: Grafana's Velero integration documentation lists the Velero Prometheus metrics used for backup, restore, and snapshot monitoring.

claims.claim_text: Velero began as Heptio Ark and was renamed Velero after Heptio joined VMware.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 1
claims.rationale: The primary VMware Tanzu announcement directly supports the acquisition/renaming context, but blogs.vmware.com is not on the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Velero's MinIO quick-start example is intended for basic evaluation, uses MinIO as an S3-compatible service inside the cluster, and is not production configuration guidance.
claims.span_hint: section: Hands-On Exercise Step 2 and Step 3
claims.rationale: The Velero MinIO quick-start says the example explores basic functionality, uses local S3-compatible MinIO, and leaves production MinIO configuration out of scope.

claims.claim_text: Velero v1.18 is expected to support Kubernetes 1.18 through latest and is tested on Kubernetes 1.35.0.
claims.span_hint: frontmatter/lab assumptions: Examples target Kubernetes 1.35+
claims.rationale: The Velero README compatibility matrix lists v1.18 expected Kubernetes compatibility and tested Kubernetes versions including 1.35.0.

further_reading.title: Velero README and Compatibility Matrix
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary upstream overview for Velero capabilities, release notes, and Kubernetes compatibility.

further_reading.title: Velero Restore Reference
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains restore workflow, namespace mapping, PV/PVC restore behavior, and restore ordering.

further_reading.title: Velero File System Backup
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers node-agent FSB behavior, opt-in/opt-out volume selection, consistency tradeoffs, and limitations.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes Volume Snapshots
further_reading.why_relevant: Authoritative Kubernetes reference for CSI VolumeSnapshot API objects and snapshot lifecycle.

notes: Code blocks and exercise-only commands were not cited except where the prose made a factual product/version claim. The AWS plugin sentence is intentionally marked cannot_be_salvaged because the pinned v1.13.0 tag is not the current plugin for Velero v1.18/Kubernetes 1.35-era examples.
